g.data-narration: Welcome to this comprehensive module on Water Resources: Availability and Quality Aspects for engineers.
g.text: Water Resources: Availability and Quality Aspects

g.data-narration: Water is essential for all life, agriculture, industry, and maintaining ecological balance. Understanding water resources is vital for sustainable development.
g.text: • Fundamental natural resource for all life forms
• Crucial for agricultural productivity, industrial development, and domestic consumption
• Essential for maintaining ecological balance and environmental protection

g.data-narration: India receives about 4000 billion cubic meters of water annually, but only 1200 billion cubic meters is utilizable due to distribution patterns.
Water resources are broadly classified into surface water (rivers, lakes, reservoirs) and groundwater (stored in underground aquifers).
g.text: Rivers, lakes, reservoirs, ponds • 90% of surface flow
Aquifers, wells, boreholes • 40% of total utilization

g.data-narration: India has 20 major river basins with major systems like Ganges, Brahmaputra, and Krishna. Over 100 reservoirs store approximately 250 billion cubic meters.
India has over 30 million groundwater structures. Annual groundwater potential is ~450 billion m³, but extraction has led to declining water tables in Punjab, Haryana, and Tamil Nadu.
India's water distribution varies spatially (north receives 2500mm, west gets less than 500mm) and temporally (75% rainfall during monsoon June-September).
Per capita water availability has declined from 5,177 m³ (1951) to ~1,400 m³ currently, placing India in the water-stressed category.
Physical parameters include turbidity (cloudiness), color, taste, odor, and temperature. BIS recommends turbidity less than 1 NTU for drinking water.
Chemical parameters include pH (6.5-8.5 for drinking water), dissolved oxygen (above 8 mg/L excellent), and BOD (below 2 mg/L desirable).
Biological parameters include coliform bacteria (indicator of fecal contamination) and bacterial count. Zero coliform is required for safe drinking water.
g.text: Zero MPN/100mL for drinking water (BIS standard) • Fecal contamination indicator

g.data-narration: Point sources have identifiable discharge points (industrial outlets, sewage). Non-point sources are diffuse (agricultural runoff, urban stormwater), harder to control.
g.text: Industrial outlets, sewage drains • Identifiable discharge points
Agricultural runoff, urban stormwater • Diffuse, harder to control
Health impacts: cholera, typhoid | Ecosystem damage | Economic losses

g.data-narration: Learn to calculate WQI and BOD. WQI = weighted mean of sub-indices. BOD = (Initial DO - Final DO) × dilution factor.
g.text: Example: pH=7.2, DO=8.5, BOD=2, TDS=350, Coliform=0
Example: Initial DO=9 mg/L, Final DO=2 mg/L
Example: 50 km², 1200mm rain, 0.3 runoff, 40% efficiency = 16.8 million m³

g.data-narration: Sustainable water management includes watershed development, rainwater harvesting, wastewater treatment, pricing, and public awareness programs.
Remember these essential formulas: WQI, BOD, groundwater recharge, and runoff volume calculations for engineering applications.
India faces water scarcity despite abundant resources. Focus on conservation, quality monitoring, and sustainable management for future water security.
g.text: • India: 16% world population, 4% freshwater • Per capita availability declining
• BIS Standards: pH 6.5-8.5, TDS <500 mg/L, Zero coliform for drinking water
• High BOD = Pollution • Point vs Non-point sources • Conservation essential!
